summary: This brief post defines annotations as meta-data attached to program elements that can embellish code with extra behavior. Fowler explains that Java calls these 'annotations' while C# calls them 'attributes,' with C# having them from its first release and Java adding them in version 1.5. He uses C#'s [Obsolete] attribute as a practical example, noting it can mark deprecated elements with custom messages and error/warning levels. Fowler states his preference for the term 'annotation' as the general term since 'attribute' is overloaded with other meanings. He also notes that some languages like Ruby implement annotations through unconventional syntax like class methods.
key_points: Annotations are meta-data on program elements (classes, methods, fields) that embellish code with extra behavior
Java uses 'annotation' terminology while C# uses 'attribute' - C# had them first, Java added them in 1.5
Language platforms provide built-in annotations but allow custom ones
The term 'annotation' is preferred over 'attribute' because 'attribute' has too many other meanings
Some languages implement annotations through non-obvious syntax patterns
money_quotes: An annotation on a program element (commonly a class, method, or field) is a piece of meta-data added to that program element which can be used to embellish that element with extra code.
When writing about programming, I prefer to use 'annotation' as the general term. Although .NET was first, the word 'attribute' is just too widely used for different things.
Languages may provide annotations in ways that don't reflect the syntax of the language.
key_insight: Annotations provide a standardized way to attach meta-data to code elements, though their implementation varies significantly across languages.
